title: The Brahma Sutras — The Weaving of the Absolute
subtitle: The four adhikaraṇas as the structure of inquiry; Brahman as the cause — compared to Śiva tattva; satkārya-vāda as emanation of the tattvas

summary.one_line: Bādarāyaṇa's Brahma Sūtras analyzed as the definitive Vedantic text weaving the Upaniṣadic utterances into a coherent system: Brahman as the material-efficient cause, the non-difference of cause an...
summary.scope: Covers the four adhyāyas (Samanvaya, Avirodha, Sādhana, Phala), the nature of Brahman, satkārya-vāda, the role of upāsanā (meditation), the nature of the individual soul, and jīvanmukti (liberation while living).

sources.label: Brahma Sūtras with Śaṅkara's Commentary (Swami Gambhirananda trans.)

body.text: [See BS I.i.1: 'Athāto brahma-jijñāsā' — 'Hence, thereafter, a deliberation on Brahman.' The four adhyāyas form a complete inquiry: (1) Samanvaya — establishing that all Upaniṣadic texts refer to Brahman, the non-dual Reality; (2) Avirodha — refuting objections from Sāṅkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Buddhist, Jaina, and other systems; (3) Sādhana — the means to release, including meditation (upāsanā), the path of the gods, and the nature of the soul; (4) Phala — the fruit, i.e., liberation, the nature of the released soul. This fourfold structure is itself a complete sādhana: theory, dialectics, practice, and realization — corresponding to the four stages of daimon work: hearing (śravaṇa), reasoning (manana), meditation (nididhyāsana), and realization (sākṣātkāra).]

[See BS I.i.2: 'Janmādy asya yataḥ' — 'That from which the origin, etc. (sustenance and dissolution) of this universe proceed.' This sutra establishes Brahman as the material and efficient cause of the universe — not a demiurge but the very substance of reality. Compare to Śiva tattva in Trika: Śiva is both the material cause (upādāna-kāraṇa) and the efficient cause (nimitta-kāraṇa) of the universe through his śakti. The difference is terminological: Brahman as sat-cit-ānanda, Śiva as prakāśa-vimarśa. Both are the one reality that becomes the world without ceasing to be itself. This is the unus-ambo as cosmic principle: one reality appearing as cause and effect without division.]

[See BS II.i.14-20. The doctrine of satkārya-vāda: the effect is non-different from the cause, pre-exists in the cause, and is a manifestation (vivarta) of the cause. 'The effect is non-different from the cause; the cause alone is real.' Śaṅkara uses the example of clay and pots: the pot is nothing but clay — its form is name-and-form (nāma-rūpa), a mere convention. The examples used — milk turning into curds (BS II.i.24), water turning into ice — all illustrate that the transformation is apparent, not real. This is exactly the Trika doctrine of ābhāsa (reflection): the tattvas are not created ex nihilo but are the self-reflections of Śiva. The daimon's relationship to the soul is the same: the soul is not separate from the daimon but is its reflection — apparently different, essentially identical.]

[See BS III.iii and IV.i.1-8. Upāsanā (meditation) is the means to liberation for those who approach Brahman through the qualified (saguṇa) aspect. The meditations on Brahman with attributes — as the person in the eye, as the space in the heart, as the internal ruler — are detailed in the third adhyāya. 'Repetition of meditations, etc.' (BS IV.i.1) prescribes the continuous practice of meditation until death (BS IV.i.8): 'Meditation till death.' The meditations are not mere mental exercises but the actual construction of the subtle body through which the soul travels the path of the gods (devayāna) to Brahma-loka. This is the daimon-path: the practitioner builds, through sustained attention, the luminous body that carries the soul beyond the cycle of rebirth. Upāsanā is the methodical unification of consciousness with its object — the unus-ambo as practice.]

[See BS II.iii.50: 'Ābhāsa eva ca' — 'And [the soul is] indeed a reflection.' The individual soul (jīva) is not a separate entity but a reflection of Brahman in the limiting adjuncts (upādhi) of the body, senses, and mind. Like the sun reflected in different vessels of water, Brahman appears as many souls. When the vessels break, the reflections merge back into the sun. This is the doctrine of pratibimba-vāda (reflectionism), which is also the Trika teaching: the soul is a reflection of Śiva in the mirror of māyā. The daimon is the original, the soul is the reflection — yet the reflection is not different from the original. The path of knowledge is the recognition that one is the original, not the reflection. This is the heart of the unus-ambo: the soul and the daimon are two views of one reality.]

[See BS IV.i.13: 'Phalam ata upapatteḥ' — 'The fruit [of knowledge is liberation] from that, for it is reasonable.' And BS IV.ii.12-16, IV.iv.22. The liberated soul (jīvanmukta) continues to live in the body until prārabdha-karma (the karma that has begun to fructify) is exhausted. 'For the knower of Brahman there is no departure [of the prāṇas]; they get resolved even here' (BS IV.ii.12). The body continues like a potter's wheel spinning after the potter stops. The jīvanmukta is the completed work: one who knows Brahman while alive, whose body is maintained by the momentum of past karma but whose consciousness is fully established in the absolute. This is the state of the Completed Work — the Great Work of the alchemists, the union with the Holy Guardian Angel, the unus-ambo realized in life.]

gaps: Detailed treatment of the dialectical arguments of Adhyāya 2 needed; comparison with Rāmānuja's commentary
Detailed catalogue of the vidyās (meditations) from Adhyāya 3 needed

issues.title: Passages need direct quotation from primary text
issues.description: Currently referencing source synthesis. Direct sutra citations from both the sūtras and Śaṅkara's bhāṣya needed.

issues.title: Missing treatment of the refutation of other schools in Adhyāya 2
issues.description: The detailed critique of Sāṅkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Buddhist, and Jaina positions is a major part of the Brahma Sūtras and needs coverage.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. 6 passages covering the four adhyāyas, Brahman as cause, satkārya-vāda, upāsanā, soul-reflection theory, and jīvanmukti.
